reasoning:
To assess the probability of North Korea testing a nuclear weapon before July 1, 2025, I examined evidence regarding North Korea's nuclear activities. The seventh nuclear test has been anticipated by South Korean and U.S. intelligence since approximately mid-2022. North Korea officially closed the Punggye-ri test site in 2018, but intelligence indicated it was prepared for a new test starting in 2022. Recently (late 2024 and early 2025), intelligence reassessed that North Korea has likely completed test preparations and is prepared to conduct a nuclear test "on short notice." In January 2025, Kim Jong-un called 2025 a "crucial year" for the country's nuclear capability and visited a nuclear material production base. 
However, despite constant warnings since 2022 that a test was imminent (e.g., surrounding the November 2024 US election), North Korea has refrained from conducting its seventh nuclear test, instead relying on ICBM and other ballistic missile tests. Under the current trajectory, Kim Jong-un seems to be using the implied threat of a nuclear test as leverage, perhaps seeing what sort of deal/diplomacy he can strike with the new US administration (Trump) rather than immediately detonating a weapon which might draw severe sanctions and alienate China and Russia—who currently support North Korea. Without a clear triggering event in the next two months (May and June 2025), the chance of a sudden test is relatively low, although the technical capability is certainly there. Given the consistent "crying wolf" since 2022 regarding an imminent test, and the lack of a successful trigger so far into 2025, I estimate the base rate of a test in any given 2-month period to be low. The probability is slightly elevated due to the explicit rhetoric for 2025 being a "crucial year," but still less than 50%. A 20% probability seems appropriate, reflecting the constant background threat and readiness, balanced against years of restraint and geopolitical calculations favoring missile tests over nuclear detonations.
